metadata.summary: Design voice interfaces by writing dialogues first, then defining intents, slots, prompts, and error-recovery before NLU training. Prevents robotic, unhelpful voice experiences.

content.title: Voice UI Design: Dialogue-First Methodology for Voice Assistants and Commands
content.summary: A methodology for designing speech-controlled interfaces — voice assistants, app voice commands, IVR systems — by writing sample dialogues first, then defining intents/slots, authoring prompts, and designing a 3-tier error-recovery ladder before any NLU training begins. Voice interfaces fail when copied from visual UI patterns: users do not know what to say, prompts are robotic, errors are dead ends. Writing dialogues before code forces natural language and exposes missing intents early. Error ladders (rephrase → examples → fallback) reduce abandonment; explicit confirmation strategy prevents irreversible actions without user awareness.

why.p: Voice interfaces fail when copied from visual UI patterns: users do not know what to say, prompts are robotic, errors are dead ends. Writing dialogues before code forces natural language and exposes missing intents early. Error ladders (rephrase → examples → fallback) reduce abandonment; explicit confirmation strategy prevents irreversible actions without user awareness.

list.item: Designing a voice assistant, Alexa/Google skill, or IVR flow end-to-end.
Adding voice commands to an existing app for hands-free or accessibility use.
Migrating a legacy NLU bot (Dialogflow, Lex) to an LLM-powered runtime.
Defining acceptance criteria for a voice feature in a spec or design doc.
Auditing transcripts of an existing voice product for verbosity or missing error paths.
Visual UI work where voice is cosmetic — invest in the core visual flow first.
Highly private data entry (financial account numbers, medical detail) without strong auth design.
Noisy or shared environments where ASR reliability cannot be guaranteed.
Browsing or exploratory tasks — visual lists outperform voice for discovery.
Rapid prototype stage before basic ASR integration is confirmed working.

rule.statement: Define all six VUI components before writing any dialogue or NLU config.
rule.rationale: Shared vocabulary prevents intent/slot confusion during team handoff and NLU training.

list.item: Wake word — activates the listener (e.g., "Alexa", "Hey Google").
Intent — the action the user wants to perform (e.g., SetTimer, OrderFood).
Slot — a typed variable within an intent (e.g., duration="15 minutes", room="living room").
Utterance — one of the many natural-language phrasings a user might say to trigger an intent.
Prompt — what the system says to the user; may be initial, confirmation, or error.
Confirmation — explicit ("Shall I place the order?") or implicit ("Adding milk to your list.") verification before action.

rule.statement: Choose voice only for tasks in the left column; route all right-column tasks to visual UI.
rule.rationale: ASR accuracy, privacy, and discovery constraints make voice unsuitable for complex or sensitive tasks.

list.item: Good for voice: hands-busy tasks, quick single-intent commands, simple queries, accessibility alternatives, multitasking contexts.
Not good for voice: complex data entry, private information, noisy environments, browsing or exploring, detailed comparison.

rule.statement: Identify tasks where hands-free or eyes-free operation provides measurable value before touching dialogue design.
rule.rationale: Designing voice for unsuitable tasks wastes NLU budget and produces frustrated users.

rule.statement: Write natural-language happy-path and error dialogues before defining intents, slots, or NLU configs.
rule.rationale: Dialogue-first design catches unnatural phrasing and missing recovery paths before any code is written.

example.title: Natural happy-path dialogues
example.code:
User: "Set a timer for 15 minutes" System: "Timer set for 15 minutes, starting now."
User: "Add milk to my shopping list" System: "I've added milk to your shopping list."

rule.statement: Each intent must have at least 5 sample utterances covering natural phrasing variations.
rule.rationale: NLU models underfit without phrasing diversity; users say the same thing dozens of different ways.

example.title: Intent definition with slot coverage
example.code:
Intent: ControlLight Slots: - room (required): "living room", "kitchen" - action (required): "on", "off", "dim"
Sample utterances: - "Turn off the living room lights" - "Lights off in the living room" - "Living room lights off" - "Switch off the living room lights" - "Kill the lights in the living room"

rule.statement: Write initial prompts as single questions; keep error prompts to one sentence plus one example.
rule.rationale: Long prompts force users to remember too much audio; concision reduces cognitive load and re-prompting.

example.title: Concise, focused prompts
example.code: Initial: "What would you like to order?" Error: "I didn't catch that. Try saying a size like 'medium' or 'large'." Confirm: "That's a large pepperoni for pickup at noon. Should I place the order?"

example.title: Verbose robotic prompts
example.code: "Hello! Welcome to Pizza Palace, the best pizza in town! We have so many great options! What can I get for you today?"
example.note: Verbose initial prompts increase time-to-first-response and are perceived as robotic.

rule.statement: Every intent must have a three-tier error strategy: rephrase → examples → fallback (agent or visual).
rule.rationale: Single-tier error handling ("Please try again") is the primary cause of user abandonment in voice.

list.item: 1st error: rephrase the original prompt more concisely.
2nd error: rephrase with concrete examples of valid input.
3rd error: offer a human agent transfer or visual alternative.

rule.statement: Run Wizard-of-Oz testing (human plays the system) before any NLU training begins.
rule.rationale: WoZ reveals what users actually say — which is often different from every utterance the team wrote.

rule.statement: Use implicit confirmation for low-risk actions; use explicit confirmation for irreversible or high-cost actions.
rule.rationale: Explicit confirmation for every action creates confirmation fatigue; skipping it for irreversible actions causes errors users cannot undo.

list.item: Low-risk reversible (add to list, set reminder) → implicit: "Adding milk to your list."
High-risk or costly (place order, delete account) → explicit: "That's $18.50. Should I place the order?"
Irreversible and high-value → explicit + repeat details before confirmation.

rule.statement: Every prompt must contain exactly one question or one instruction, never both.
rule.rationale: Dual-purpose prompts force users to choose which part to answer, causing intent mismatch.

rule.statement: Reprompts must be shorter than the original prompt and progress in specificity: shorter → with examples → with fallback offer.
rule.rationale: Identical reprompts produce the same ASR error; each tier must change the information available to the user.

antipattern.description: LLM-based VUI confirms an action ("Done!") without verifying the back-end succeeded.
antipattern.reason: Latency and API errors mean the action may have failed; hallucinated confirmations erode trust irreparably.

antipattern.description: Total round-trip latency over 1.5 seconds without streaming audio.
antipattern.reason: Users interpret gaps beyond 1.5 s as system failure and start speaking again, causing overlapping ASR input.

antipattern.description: Utterance logs retained without a privacy/PII policy defined in the design phase.
antipattern.reason: Voice utterances contain sensitive context; retention policy must be a design constraint, not an afterthought.

antipattern.description: No "endpointing" visual cue — the user cannot see if the system is listening.
antipattern.reason: Without a visible or auditory listening indicator, users do not know when to speak, causing silence timeouts.

list.item: ASR (speech recognition) accuracy degrades with non-native accents, kids, elderly voices.
LLM-based VUIs hallucinate confirmations ("Done!") without verifying the back-end action.
Latency budget: total round-trip > 1.5s feels broken; LLMs typically push past it without streaming.
Reprompts pile up if the user is interrupted by a noise — design needs barge-in handling.
Privacy: utterance logs contain PII; retention policy must be in design, not an afterthought.
Without a visible "ear", users don't know if the system is listening — endpointing UX is critical.
Task completion rate — did the user accomplish their goal without fallback?
Turns to complete — how many exchanges were needed? (target: 1-2 for simple commands)
Error rate — % of turns that triggered a reprompt.
Fallback rate — % of sessions that hit the 3rd-tier error fallback.
CSAT — post-interaction satisfaction score.

section.name: agentic-workflow
section.p: Use a subagent to author sample dialogues from a use-case spec, then expand each dialogue into intents, slots, primary prompts, reprompt ladders, and confirmation strategy. A second agent runs Wizard-of-Oz simulation by reading typed user inputs and replying with prompt outputs, generating realistic transcripts to stress-test the design. Human review for any irreversible action prompts.

list.item: faion-usability-agent — drafts dialogues, prompts, error reprompts, confirmation matrix.
faion-ux-researcher-agent — runs Wizard-of-Oz testing, accent diversity recruitment.
faion-sdd-executor-agent — implements on Alexa SDK / Dialogflow CX / Rasa / OpenAI Realtime.

section.name: prompt-patterns
section.code: For feature <feature>, write 3 happy-path dialogues, 2 error dialogues, and 1 fallback dialogue. Use natural English, no marketing tone. Mark each system turn with intent + slot table.
Given intent <name> with slots <slot list>, generate 8 utterance variants spanning command, polite, terse, and conversational register. List any utterance that would collide with intent <other>.

list.item: ask-cli — Alexa Skill lifecycle. Install: `npm i -g ask-cli`. https://developer.amazon.com/alexa
gactions — Google Conversational Actions. https://developers.google.com/assistant/conversational/gactions
dialogflow (gcloud component) — Dialogflow CX agents. https://cloud.google.com/dialogflow/cx/docs
rasa — OSS dialogue framework. Install: `pip install rasa`. https://rasa.com
voice2json — Offline NLU prototyping. https://voice2json.org
OpenAI Realtime / Whisper CLI — Streaming ASR + LLM voice. https://platform.openai.com/docs/guides/realtime
pipecat — Voice-agent pipeline framework. https://github.com/pipecat-ai/pipecat
Alexa Skills Kit (SaaS) — Yes, ASK SDK + CLI. Certification still human-gated.
Dialogflow CX / ES (SaaS) — Yes, REST + CLI. Strong slot filling.
Amazon Lex v2 (SaaS) — Yes, CLI + CloudFormation. Ties to AWS infra.
Rasa (OSS) — Yes, fully scriptable. Best for self-hosted, on-prem.
Microsoft Copilot Studio (SaaS) — Partial, REST API. Power Platform tie-in.
LiveKit Agents (OSS) — Yes, Python SDK. Best for real-time voice agents.
ElevenLabs / OpenAI TTS (SaaS) — Yes, REST. Voice synthesis quality bench.
Vapi / Retell AI (SaaS) — Yes, REST. Phone-based voice agents.

section.name: minimal-rasa-example
section.code: version: "3.1" nlu: - intent: set_timer examples: | - set a timer for [15 minutes](duration) - set a [15 minute](duration) timer - timer [15 min](duration) - start a [pasta](name) timer for [10 minutes](duration) - countdown [90 seconds](duration) responses: utter_timer_confirm: - text: "Timer set for {duration}, starting now." utter_timer_reprompt_no_input: - text: "How long should the timer run?" utter_timer_fallback: - text: "I didn't catch that. Try saying '15 minutes' or 'half an hour'."

list.item: Write dialogues before intents — design follows speech, not the other way around.
Cap a single prompt at ~14 words; long prompts get interrupted.
Implicit confirmation ("Adding milk to your list") for low-risk; explicit for high-risk + irreversible.
Reprompt ladder: 1) rephrase, 2) examples, 3) fallback (visual / human / alternative).
Always include a `cancel` / `nevermind` intent at every turn.
Stream TTS as soon as the first phrase is decided — do not wait for full response.
Test with at least 5 accents and 2 age groups before launch.
LLMs hallucinate "Done." without verifying the action; require the agent to call the tool first, then narrate.
Agents under-generate utterance variants — force minimum 5 across registers.
Slot validation: agents accept anything; require a typed schema (duration: ISO8601, currency: ISO4217).
Endpointing in LLM voice agents: agent must support barge-in or users will talk over it.
Privacy: agent must redact PII in logged transcripts before storing.
Confirmation drift: an LLM rephrases "place the order" softer each turn — pin the wording.

template.purpose: Voice flow doc: overview, happy-path dialogue, intent definitions, error handling, fallbacks.
Per-prompt spec: primary text, short/long variants, reprompts 1-3, A/B variants.
